# **Theoretical Foundations and Algorithmic Frameworks for Optimizing Diversified Ensemble Selection in LLM Hallucination Detection**

The detection of hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) has transitioned from a niche evaluative challenge to a primary bottleneck in the deployment of generative agents within high-stakes industrial and clinical environments.1 Current state-of-the-art frameworks for hallucination detection often rely on self-probing mechanisms, where the model's internal confidence is quantified through scalar metrics such as the Entropy Production Rate (EPR).3 While single-view detection provides a foundational baseline, the inherent stochasticity of autoregressive decoding necessitates the use of ensemble-based strategies to improve robustness.5 A multi-view approach, which aggregates predictions from diverse prompt templates (views), seeks to leverage the "wisdom of crowds" to identify inconsistencies that a single prompt might obscure.5

A critical implementation challenge in these systems is the "negative lift" phenomenon, where the performance of an integrated ensemble falls below that of its strongest individual constituent. This failure is frequently attributed to high semantic correlation among the selected views, leading to redundant error patterns that bias the fusion process.6 In the context of Nadler spectral fusion—a principled, unsupervised aggregation method—the assumption of conditional independence among base learners is paramount for identifying the latent "truth".9 When this assumption is violated due to excessive similarity in prompt logic, the resulting EPR scores become highly correlated, causing the spectral decomposition to fail in its task of accurately weighting individual predictors.10

This report provides an exhaustive investigation into subset selection algorithms, continuous optimization strategies, and information-theoretic frameworks designed to resolve the quality-diversity tension in view selection. By exploring principled methodologies such as submodular maximization, determinantal point processes (DPPs), and Bayesian experimental design (BED), it is possible to construct ensemble detectors that maximize the fused Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC-AUC) curve while maintaining computational efficiency in standard development environments.13

## **Mathematical Foundations of Nadler Spectral Fusion and the Consistency Problem**

Nadler spectral fusion, primarily operationalized through the Spectral Meta-Learner (SML) and the Strategy for Unsupervised Multiple Method Aggregation (SUMMA), provides a rigorous mathematical basis for aggregating multiple binary or continuous scoring functions without requiring ground-truth labels during inference.9 The core objective of these methods is to recover the performance parameters of each individual "view" (such as sensitivity, specificity, or AUC) by analyzing the structure of the covariance matrix of their predictions.10

### **The Rank-One Covariance Assumption**

The theoretical validity of spectral fusion rests on the assumption that the ![][image1] base learners make errors that are conditionally independent given the true label ![][image2].9 Let ![][image3] represent the EPR score generated by view ![][image4] for a given question-answer pair ![][image5]. When these scores are normalized to have zero mean, the population covariance ![][image6] for ![][image7] can be decomposed into a product of the conditional expectations 11:

![][image8]  
where ![][image9] is the class prevalence, and ![][image10] represents the expected score of view ![][image4] given the true class ![][image11].17 This structure implies that the off-diagonal entries of the covariance matrix ![][image12] form a rank-one matrix.9 The leading eigenvector of this adjusted matrix provides entries proportional to the individual accuracies of the views, allowing the system to assign optimal weights for linear fusion.11

The phenomenon of negative lift occurs when views are semantically similar. Similarity introduces a "nuisance" term into the off-diagonal entries of the covariance matrix, representing the shared error distribution of correlated prompts.10 Mathematically, if view ![][image4] and view ![][image13] are highly correlated, the covariance becomes:

![][image14]  
where ![][image15] represents the error term and ![][image16] is the performance metric.10 If ![][image17], the spectral method overestimates the quality of these views, leading the fusion to prioritize redundant information over diverse perspectives.9 Consequently, selecting views that maximize individual AUC while minimizing this error covariance is essential for a principled solution to the hallucination detection problem.

### **Implications of Sample Size and Class Imbalance**

The consistency of the spectral estimator is also governed by the number of labeled questions ![][image18] and the degree of class imbalance ![][image19].9 Research indicates that the spectral meta-learner converges at a rate of ![][image20], meaning that with ![][image21], the estimation of view weights is generally stable, provided the views are sufficiently diverse.9 However, if the hallucination rate ![][image19] is extremely low (highly imbalanced data), the signal-to-noise ratio in the covariance matrix drops, making the selection of high-performing, non-redundant views even more critical.9

| Parameter | Theoretical Impact on Nadler Fusion | Practical Constraint (N=200,K=4) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| View Correlation | Violates rank-one assumption; biases weights | Primary cause of negative lift |
| Sample Size (![][image18]) | Governs convergence rate (![][image20]) | Sufficient for stable spectral estimation |
| Number of Views (![][image22]) | Affects degrees of freedom in covariance | ![][image23] is minimal; requires high-quality inputs |
| Class Prevalence (![][image19]) | Determines the magnitude of the rank-one signal | Moderate imbalance is manageable; extreme imbalance requires higher ![][image18] |

## **Principled Algorithms for Quality-Diversity Subset Selection**

The task of selecting ![][image23] views from a pool of ![][image24] candidates is a combinatorial optimization problem that can be framed as a search for a subset that maximizes a joint utility function.14 This utility function must account for the individual predictive power of the views (quality) and their relative independence (diversity).14

### **Submodular Maximization and the Greedy Approximation**

Submodularity is a mathematical property of set functions that formalizes the intuitive concept of diminishing returns.14 A function ![][image25] is submodular if the marginal gain of adding an element to a small set is greater than or equal to the marginal gain of adding the same element to a larger set.14 In diversity-aware selection, a common objective is:

![][image26]  
where ![][image27] is a regularization parameter and ![][image28] is a similarity metric.14 While this specific form is not always monotone submodular, many related functions—such as the Maximum Marginal Relevance (MMR) objective—can be optimized using a greedy algorithm with strong theoretical guarantees.14

The standard greedy algorithm starts with an empty set and iteratively adds the view that maximizes the marginal utility.14 For monotone submodular functions, this greedy approach is guaranteed to provide a solution that is at least ![][image29] of the optimal solution.14 For a configuration with ![][image24] and ![][image23], the computational complexity of ![][image30] is trivial, making it highly suitable for Colab-based environments.13

### **Determinantal Point Processes (DPPs) for Global Diversity**

Determinantal Point Processes provide a more sophisticated alternative to greedy pairwise optimization.8 Instead of considering only pairwise disagreements, a DPP defines a probability distribution over all possible subsets, where the probability of a subset ![][image31] is proportional to the determinant of a kernel matrix ![][image32] restricted to ![][image31] 13:

![][image33]  
The kernel matrix ![][image32] is typically constructed such that its diagonal elements ![][image34] represent the quality (individual AUC) of view ![][image4], and its off-diagonal elements ![][image35] represent the similarity between views ![][image4] and ![][image13].13 The determinant of ![][image36] geometrically represents the squared volume of the parallelepiped spanned by the feature vectors of the views in ![][image31].8 If views are highly similar, their vectors are nearly parallel, the volume collapses, and the determinant approaches zero.8

For subset selection, the goal is to find the subset that maximizes this determinant (Maximum A Posteriori or MAP inference).13 While finding the global maximum is NP-hard, a greedy MAP approximation is highly effective and runs in ![][image37] time.13 For ![][image23], this calculation is nearly instantaneous.13

### **Feature Selection with Redundancy Penalties (mRMR)**

The Minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance (mRMR) algorithm is a classic technique in feature selection that aligns closely with the view selection problem.20 mRMR selects features that have high mutual information with the target label while having low mutual information with already selected features.23 In the hallucination detection context, mutual information can be replaced by the Spearman rank correlation of the continuous EPR scores.11

| Algorithm | Optimization Strategy | Diversity Metric | Theoretical Guarantee | Scale (M=50,K=4) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Greedy Submodular | Marginal Gain | Pairwise Similarity | ![][image38] | Highly Feasible |
| DPP Greedy MAP | Determinant Maximization | Volume Span | Probabilistic spread | Highly Feasible |
| mRMR | ![][image39] | Mutual Information | Heuristic / Submodular | Highly Feasible |
| Exhaustive Search | ![][image40] | Direct Fusion AUC | Exact Optimal | Feasible (![][image41] iter) |

The complexity analysis suggests that while ![][image42] combinations is technically feasible in a few minutes of Python execution, the principled algorithms (Greedy Submodular/DPP) are more robust as ![][image1] scales towards 100 or when the evaluation of the fusion AUC requires cross-validation, which would make the exhaustive search significantly more expensive.12

## **Analyzing the Submodularity of the Nadler Fusion Objective**

A pivotal question in the optimization of view ensembles is whether the ROC-AUC of the fused scores is a submodular function of the set of selected views. If the objective is submodular, the greedy algorithm is near-optimal; if not, greedy selection may be significantly suboptimal, potentially explaining the observed negative lift.14

### **Submodularity of ROC-AUC**

In supervised classification, ROC-AUC is generally *not* submodular.18 The addition of a new feature (view) to an existing set can have varying marginal effects depending on whether the new feature is complementary to the current set's decision boundaries.26 In some cases, the inclusion of a third or fourth view can significantly clarify the separation between classes that was ambiguous to the first two, leading to a marginal gain larger than what the view would have provided individually—a direct violation of the diminishing returns property.26

### **Proxies and Surrogates in Unsupervised Fusion**

In the unsupervised Nadler setting, the fusion AUC is a function of the leading eigenvector of the covariance matrix.10 While the final AUC might not be submodular, many of its underlying components are.11 For instance:

* **Log-Determinant of Covariance:** Maximizing ![][image43] is a monotone submodular task.13 This objective encourages selecting views that are linearly independent, which is exactly the requirement for a stable spectral decomposition.10  
* **Mutual Information:** The information gain regarding the true label from a set of conditionally independent views is a submodular function.16

The implication for the current disagreement\_select algorithm is that its poor performance may not be due to the greedy approach itself, but rather the choice of a suboptimal diversity proxy (binarized disagreement) that fails to capture the continuous relationships required for Nadler fusion.10

## **Diversity Metrics for Continuous EPR Scores**

The current methodology binarizes EPR scores at their mean to compute disagreement, a process that discards the fine-grained probabilistic information inherent in LLM uncertainty.29 Several continuous metrics are better suited for this setting, providing a more granular view of redundancy.

### **Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC)**

The Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC) is a kernel-based statistic that measures the generalized dependence between two continuous random variables.19 Unlike simple correlation, which only detects linear relationships, HSIC can identify complex non-linear dependencies by mapping the scores into a high-dimensional Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS).19

Two views are statistically independent if and only if their HSIC is zero.19 For ![][image18] samples, the empirical HSIC is calculated as:

![][image44]  
where ![][image45] and ![][image46] are kernel matrices (e.g., Gaussian/RBF) of the two score vectors, and ![][image47] is a centering matrix.30 Using HSIC as the redundancy penalty in an mRMR or greedy framework allows the selection process to penalize views that are semantically similar even if their linear correlation is low.19

### **Volumetric and Determinantal Metrics**

Maximizing the determinant of the score covariance matrix is another powerful way to use continuous information.8 This metric, central to DPPs, treats each view as a vector in ![][image48].8 The determinant measures the volume of the space "covered" by the views.15 High volume indicates that the views are making their "confession" of uncertainty in different regions of the question space, providing the spectral fusion algorithm with a truly multi-perspective data source.9

### **Rank Correlation (Spearman's Rho)**

For the SUMMA framework, which is specifically designed for ranked predictions, the Spearman rank correlation is the most natural diversity metric.11 It measures the monotonicity of the relationship between scores without assuming linearity, making it more robust to the non-Gaussian tails often seen in EPR distributions.26

| Metric | Type | Sensitivity | Computational Effort | Suitability for EPR |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Binarized Disagreement | Binary | Low (Discards info) | Minimal | Suboptimal |
| Pearson Correlation | Continuous | Linear only | Minimal | Moderate |
| Spearman Rank | Continuous | Monotonic | Low | High |
| HSIC | Continuous | General (Non-linear) | Moderate (![][image49]) | Best |
| Determinant | Set-based | Global Structure | Low (![][image50]) | Best |

## **Continuous Optimization and Soft Prompt Design**

Beyond selecting from a fixed candidate pool ![][image1], there is the possibility of directly optimizing the prompt space to generate diverse views.8 This shifts the problem from discrete subset selection to continuous manifold optimization.35

### **Diversity-Quality Optimization (DQO) with Soft Prompts**

Soft prompts involve prepending a learnable vector of continuous embeddings to the input question instead of using fixed natural language text.7 This allows for the direct application of gradient-based optimization.8 In a "repulsion" framework, one could learn ![][image22] soft prompts ![][image51] by minimizing a joint loss:

![][image52]  
where ![][image53] is a similarity measure like the dot product or HSIC of the resulting score vectors.8 This process forces the soft prompts to migrate to different areas of the embedding space, essentially training them to become "orthogonal" detectors.8

### **Gradient-Based Search in Instruction Space**

If fixed instruction templates are required, one can use the gradient of the diversity-aware objective in the embedding space of a sentence encoder (like CLIP or SBERT) to guide the search.35 By identifying the "direction" in the instruction embedding space that would maximize the determinant of the ensemble covariance, one can retrieve the nearest discrete prompt from a massive unlabelled corpus.35 This "discrete-to-continuous-to-discrete" loop is highly effective for discovering views that are semantically distinct but structurally valid.35

### **Learned View Generation**

Recent work on "automated prompt engineering" suggests that an LLM can be prompted to "generate a view that is maximally different from the following set of prompts".38 By providing the current set of ![][image54] views and the labeled questions where the current ensemble fails, a second model can act as a "diverse view generator".5 This utilizes the model's own semantic understanding of the tasks to create instructions that probe different reasoning paths.2

## **Bayesian and Information-Theoretic Framing of View Selection**

The problem of selecting optimal views can be rigorously framed as a problem of Bayesian Experimental Design (BED).28 In this framing, each view is an "experiment" designed to reduce uncertainty about the hidden state ![][image55].16

### **Expected Information Gain (EIG)**

The BED-LLM approach suggests selecting views that maximize the Expected Information Gain (EIG).16 The EIG of a view ![][image5] is defined as the expected reduction in the entropy of the belief distribution over the label ![][image56] 16:

![][image57]  
where ![][image58] is the observed EPR score.16 A key insight from this literature is that maximizing EIG is *not* equivalent to selecting views with high individual accuracy (low entropy ![][image59]).16 Instead, EIG favors views where the *marginal entropy* of the scores ![][image60] is high but the *conditional entropy* ![][image59] is low.16 This ensures that the view's output is highly variable across different questions but highly predictable if the true class is known—the definition of a good detector.16

### **Sequential vs. Batch Selection**

In a batch setting, BED-LLM can be used to select ![][image22] views that maximize the *joint* EIG.16 This naturally handles redundancy: if two views ![][image61] and ![][image62] provide the same information about ![][image56], the joint EIG will be only marginally higher than the individual EIG, causing the algorithm to prefer a diverse second view even if its individual accuracy is slightly lower.16

### **Online and Adaptive Selection**

Adaptive selection—where the next view is chosen based on the score obtained from the previous one—offers a more powerful but more computationally expensive alternative.16 For a specific question, if the first view returns an EPR score that is highly ambiguous (e.g., exactly at the hallucination threshold), the BED-LLM framework can select a second view specifically designed to resolve ambiguity in that part of the feature space.28 This "routing" approach can achieve much higher accuracy with fewer views than a fixed ensemble.40

## **Feasibility and implementation Strategy for Colab Environments**

The constraints of ![][image63] allow for several advanced implementations that would be impractical for larger datasets or larger ensembles.13

### **Implementing Greedy DPP in NumPy**

The Greedy MAP algorithm for DPPs is a prime candidate for this setting.13 It can be implemented in approximately 50 lines of NumPy code:

1. **Relevance Vector**: Compute the ROC-AUC for each of the ![][image1] views.  
2. **Similarity Matrix**: Compute a ![][image64] similarity matrix ![][image65] where ![][image66] is the Spearman correlation between view ![][image4] and view ![][image13].11  
3. **Kernel Construction**: ![][image67].13  
4. **Greedy Selection**: Iteratively add the view that maximizes the marginal log-determinant. This can be done efficiently using the matrix inversion lemma (Sherman-Morrison) or simply by re-calculating the determinant for the few remaining candidates at each step, given ![][image22] is so small.13

### **Implementing HSIC-mRMR**

An HSIC-based mRMR algorithm is also highly feasible 30:

1. **Pre-compute Kernels**: For each view, compute its ![][image68] kernel matrix ![][image69]. With ![][image70], this is a ![][image71] matrix—trivial for a GPU.19  
2. **Greedy Loop**:  
   * Initialize ![][image72].  
   * For ![][image73] to 4: Find view ![][image74] that maximizes ![][image75].14  
3. **Efficiency**: The trace of product of kernel matrices is an ![][image49] operation, which is very fast for ![][image70].19

### **Comparison of Continuous and Binarized Approaches**

| Feature | Current (Binarized Disagreement) | Proposed (HSIC / DPP) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Information Retention | Low (Binary thresholding) | High (Continuous EPR values) |
| Theoretical Basis | Heuristic | Statistical Independence / Volumetric Spread |
| Complexity | ![][image76] | ![][image77] or ![][image78] |
| Implementation | \< 50 lines | \~80 lines |
| Expected Performance | Negative Lift Risk | Principled Diversity Guarantee |

## **Synthesized Insights and Strategic Recommendations**

The analysis of the literature and the specific constraints of the hallucination detection problem lead to several high-level insights regarding the optimal construction of multi-view ensembles.

### **The Superiority of Global Diversity Over Pairwise Disagreement**

The transition from a greedy disagreement-based selection to a DPP-based or HSIC-based selection is likely to be the most impactful change.13 Pairwise metrics only consider how two views differ, but global metrics (like the determinant) account for the "total information volume" of the set.8 This is critical for Nadler fusion, which effectively tries to "triangulate" the truth; redundant views add noise to this triangulation, whereas truly diverse views (orthogonal in score space) clarify the latent signal.9

### **The Role of Non-Linearity in EPR Score Spaces**

LLM EPR scores are rarely linear in their relationship to the probability of correctness.3 They often exhibit threshold effects or non-monotonic behavior.29 HSIC is uniquely suited to this because it identifies independence in an RKHS, allowing it to "see" that two views might have the same linear trend but capture different error modes.30 If view A is sensitive to factual errors and view B is sensitive to logical reasoning errors, their linear correlation might still be high because both generally track "hallucination," but HSIC will recognize them as independent detectors.19

### **Exploiting the Small K Regime**

The constraint of ![][image23] is not a limitation but an opportunity.13 In large ensemble problems (![][image79]), one is forced to use simple, fast metrics.22 With ![][image23], the overhead of computing the full exhaustive AUC for all ![][image80] combinations—or using complex Bayesian Expected Information Gain estimators—is entirely manageable.12 The student's goal should be to maximize the *quality* of the diversity metric rather than the *speed* of the selection algorithm.13

### **Recommended Approaches for the Master's Thesis**

Based on technical rigor and implementation feasibility in a Colab notebook, the following three approaches are recommended:

1. **Greedy DPP MAP with Spearman Rank Kernel**: This is the most principled way to utilize the continuous nature of the scores.11 It directly optimizes the "volume" of the information provided to the Nadler fusion algorithm.9  
2. **HSIC-mRMR**: This provides the strongest statistical guarantee of independence.23 By using a Gaussian kernel, the system can penalize any form of non-linear dependency between the EPR scores of different views.19  
3. **Continuous Soft Prompt Tuning (Repulsion Loss)**: If the fixed pool of ![][image1] views is insufficient, this approach allows for the "discovery" of new, orthogonal views.8 Using a simple PyTorch loop, the student can learn soft prompt embeddings that are forced to be diverse via a determinantal or HSIC penalty term.8

## **Final Conclusion on Ensembling for Hallucination Detection**

The integration of multiple model perspectives via Nadler spectral fusion is a powerful strategy for mitigating LLM hallucinations, but its effectiveness is entirely dependent on the diversity of the inputs.9 The current "negative lift" is a consequence of selecting semantically redundant prompts that violate the conditional independence assumption of the spectral meta-learner.6 By moving from heuristic disagreement-based selection to principled algorithms like Determinantal Point Processes and HSIC-based redundancy optimization, researchers can ensure that each view in the ensemble contributes unique, non-overlapping information.13 These methods are not only mathematically robust but also highly efficient for the sample and candidate sizes typical of modern LLM evaluation tasks, fitting easily within the computational constraints of a standard single-GPU development environment.13 This transformation from a discrete selection problem to a continuous independence optimization problem represents the most promising path forward for the student's thesis work.

27. ROC as feature selection \- Cross Validated \- Stats StackExchange, accessed April 12, 2026, [https://stats.stackexchange.com/questions/261671/roc-as-feature-selection](https://stats.stackexchange.com/questions/261671/roc-as-feature-selection)

30. HSIC, accessed April 12, 2026, [https://jejjohnson.github.io/research\_journal/appendix/similarity/hsic/](https://jejjohnson.github.io/research_journal/appendix/similarity/hsic/)  
31. Beyond Pointwise Scores: A New Framework for Evaluating LLMs in High-Stakes Domains, accessed April 12, 2026, [https://medium.com/tr-labs-ml-engineering-blog/beyond-pointwise-scores-a-new-framework-for-evaluating-llms-in-high-stakes-domains-ceabb3457df9](https://medium.com/tr-labs-ml-engineering-blog/beyond-pointwise-scores-a-new-framework-for-evaluating-llms-in-high-stakes-domains-ceabb3457df9)

Human-like object concept representations emerge naturally in multimodal large language models \- ResearchGate, accessed April 12, 2026, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/381882965\_Human-like\_object\_concept\_representations\_emerge\_naturally\_in\_multimodal\_large\_language\_models](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/381882965_Human-like_object_concept_representations_emerge_naturally_in_multimodal_large_language_models)
